Alright, this is the second step. We call it the avatar, which basically it is your prospect. We are trying to make manifest who it is you're having this sales conversation with. Most good writers have an image of who they're writing to in mind. Yes, your market may be both male and female, maybe slightly older people, slightly younger people, there may be all kinds of different demographics going on. However, to get a basic idea, and I will explain this in a second, of who you're going after, you want to nail it down as much as you can to a single type of person that you're going to go after. Basically it's because you are directing your sales message to a real human being one at a time in a real conversation. I don't care if you're on the radio, on TV, if you're sending out direct mail, if you're online, if you're sending out email, your message is only going to be consumed one at a time. Your sales message is like your little tiny salesman that's going out there into the world and having a conversation with someone to convince them or persuade them or let them know that something is available. So it's not a group, it's not a faceless crowd you're going after. You're not going after pet owners, you're not going after golfers. You're going after a specific person. As I put here, not all ants, but your aunt Susie, not all golfers, but your pal Bob with all of his problems and idiosyncrasies. I'll give you a good example of that in just a second. The thing is the more you know about her or him, the more focused and more effective that conversation will be and the more you will sell. So who are you writing or speaking to? Basically who you're selling to, try to find out where do they live? What are the average demographic ranges as best as you can tell? Sometimes you have to guess at part of this, sometimes you have a range, but you'll hear marketers talk about ages 18 to 25 or ages 35 to 50. The reason they're talking about that is because they have researched who has bought their stuff and if you're a small business person you can just look through this. I've got 100 people who are pretty much middle age to a bot and I've got 400 people who are still in college in their 20s who have bought. So you start to get an idea of who the average person that you're selling to is. This is the important part. One of the keys to selling is empathy, is walking a mile in their shoes, is understanding from your prospect's point of view what the world looks like, what their needs are, where it hurts and most importantly what do they need to hear from you to feel good about buying? Some people are just waiting for what you have and all they need to know is that what you have is available and they will want to buy it. Other people need to be persuaded. They need to know that yours is the best, yours is also the biggest bargain or it's the cheapest or it presents the most value to them. There are different things that people need and if you go out and try to sell whatever it is you have to people without knowing what they need to know to be able to make that buying decision you're going to make a lot of mistakes. So figuring out who it is you're talking to and where they are in the world, what their day is like, how old they are, what their worries are, are they worried about money, are they worried about politics, are they worried about the economy, do they pay attention to sports, are they worried about embarrassing themselves in front of someone else. Let's take a look at a couple of ads. This ad is one I did many years ago for a golf client. I'm still getting royalties on this decades later by the way because once you hit the sweet spot of your avatar, once you understand who you're writing to and you get the message to them it is timeless. So this ad started out for example in direct mail, moved over to the magazines when the web became a viable marketing option, it became an online vehicle, it's been used in direct mail, a lot of different things. It just shifts with the various marketing vehicles that we've used. I want to use this example for figuring out who it was I was writing to. In the golf market, most golf advertising that you will see in say the magazines, and this ran in the magazines, most of the ads you will see assume that golfers are young, supple, they have a windmill swing and they can hit the ball fairly straight and they have a lot of things going for them. That's why you get professionals say I'm going to teach you how to hit the ball farther or have a better swing. We did some research into the golf market and we found out that the average golfer can't break a hundred to save his life. They're older, they tend to have arthritis and a lot of other problems. Going after them as if they were young athletic types who had the game nailed was the wrong way to go. Any ad approaching golfers making that assumption was going to go way over their head. What I did was, and this is a headline that we use, and it says shoulder surgery took all my strength away and shortened my swing by half. I was forced to discover the true secret of power golf. As a result, I now hit the ball farther at age 60 than I did as a long ball champion at age 28. Who I was going after was the average golfer out there which doesn't have a windmill swing which no matter how good they hit the ball ten years ago they ain't hitting it that well anymore. Life has changed, the game has changed for them. I will have a number of examples from this particular client that I had in the golf market. Not because there's anything special about golf but there was something special about the client. The client did something that most clients didn't do over the years. Most clients I had liked to step on my ads. They would change things, they got very nervous about running certain ads and they changed things and what we call stepped on the ads way too much. This client said come to work with us and we will take the leash off. We will mail or put into print advertising or put online anything you write because we trust you to do the right thing. They knew that even though some of the ads I wrote made them nervous before they went out they knew it was the right thing to do because after a couple of years they realized that when they were very nervous when I wrote an ad it was because I was hitting the sweet spot of the market and they were going to make a lot of money. So I've used a lot of golf ads here. One of the big breakthroughs here was not going after the golfer as if there's some kind of athletic person but rather an older person which they are. This is an example from a client who was going after dirt bike riding, professional dirt bike riding. The competition out there was treating dirt bike riders as if they had endless supplies of money and resources and we're just making a lot of assumptions that they were able to go wherever the competitions were that money was not a problem essentially. What I found out was that that was not the case. There were a lot of people that were kind of bootstrapping it. There were a lot of people that didn't trust the manufacturers who were putting out fancier and fancier and more expensive equipment. So the headline here is, Astonishing Insider Shortcut Secrets to Instantly Faster Times and Total Bike Control, Finally Revealed by the One Expert Mini Motocross Pros Want to Keep Hidden. Now the thing that I learned was of all the things that they wanted to learn in this particular market, Faster Times and Total Bike Control was the number one thing that was triggering responses in the ads. However, this is the thing I want to point out and you should be able to read this in your workbook even if you can't see it here. But here is something all the fat cat manufacturers and advertisers don't want you to know. When you finally learn the real secrets of blending sports science and quote dirt eating unquote pro level skills dot dot dot, you can win riding buck naked on a clapped out six year old two stroke. Now the beauty of that subhead is that it went to the heart of things. I actually as a rider did not know how to describe bikes so I went during my research I asked what's the worst kind of situation you can think of to be in a race like this. And they would say oh it's riding a old two stroke engine and when they get old like a year old bike is considered ancient in the sport. So calling it a six year old clapped out two stroke clapped out means that it's pretty beaten up. So that was a shortcut way to identify the people who understood that they were not beholden to better and more and more expensive equipment because you can win riding buck naked as opposed to the guys who were wearing what essentially were spacesuits with helmets with radio transmitters in them. There was a lot of stuff going on with the average guy couldn't afford to keep up with the guys who had unlimited resources. So by knowing this I put in this key thing and this was the response this was the part of the ad that people were really responding to. So by understanding the avatar I was able to write directly to them in a way where they understood because I knew where it hurt. I knew what they needed to hear from me that we weren't another big corporation trying to squeeze more money out of you. But rather we were a we were just like you essentially. So this is one last example. This was something I wrote for Rodale Press one of the largest direct mailers in the world. Why does this Christian doctor urgently need to speak with you about your health. So this was a product for a information book by a doctor who wanted to reach a faith based audience. So this is a basic headline that could be changed. It could be why does this overweight doctor urgently need to speak with you about your weight. Why does it doesn't even need to be doctor. Why does this overweight golfer urgently need to speak with you about your game. So it's the idea of of getting inside the head of the avatar. So the conversation that you're beginning is part of the conversation that is already in their head. This goes to people who are in looking for for health advice or have something wrong and their concerns involved whether or not the doctor is Christian or not. Or in another instance whether the doctor has actually experienced weight problems themselves or not. So this is a good example. So that's that's that kind of wraps up the avatar part. Let's go to the Q&A. All right. Welcome to the Q&A section of the your avatar module. Again I'm just going to throw John some of the most common and some of the best questions we've gathered over the tens of thousands we've seen over the years. Before I do that I do want to just restate something John said quickly when he was doing the lecture. John will frequently during the rest of the course refer to ads he's written as examples. Sometimes depending on the size of the screen you're watching on you're not going to be able to read the ad on the screen. Don't worry about it. Every ad that John refers to it'll either be in your workbook or somewhere on the website that the training site you're watching this on. So it's more important to listen to John get the message of what he's trying to say and then you're going to have the copy of all the ad to look at not just the section or the couple words he might be referring to. You're going to have the whole ad to look at. All right John but there is the number one question we get from people. 30% of people who have taken this will come back with something like but my product is for everybody. Everyone who draws breath on the planet I'm trying to sell this to. So everyone's my avatar. What do we try to help those people understand. Believe it or not I've actually encountered that with businessmen who should know better. People are in markets who say well this is for everybody and the actual answer is no it's not and that's a good place to start. Everybody who has ever existed on the face of the earth is your market. No you're going to start narrowing that down a little bit. So basically what you want to do is to understand who you're going after. People may have heard about percentages that you that you're after in direct marketing percentages like one to two percent of markets where we're talking about it doesn't matter what the percentages are that you're reaching who will become buyers. If you have a hundred people or a thousand people or a hundred thousand people in your market a certain percentage are going to buy. Another percentage are going to ignore everything you do and another percentage are going to be maybe openly hostile to what you're doing and finally there will be a big part who are maybe okay with your message but aren't going to buy right now they need to be reached multiple times. So you're not dealing with everybody even in your market in your tight little market of say golfers you know you're not going to try to sell golfing equipment to people who don't golf for example. So the answer to that to anyone who says but my product is for everybody no it's not. There are people who have specific needs who you're not you haven't thought about hard enough. What is it that they need that your product or your service is going to fix solve help or what. So once you start figuring that out then you start to narrow things down into a very manageable kind of avatar target that you can go after. Yeah I think again that we try to emphasize how you don't have a lot to learn from companies that have billions of dollars and have been at this for hundreds some years and have all their distribution is nailed down. But even if you do part of going through the simple writing system is you're going to get a lot more critical about watching ads. I know that was part of my process coming in working which on years ago I knew effectively do nothing about advertising other than like most people I'd seen lots of ads so I assume I must know something about it which I've since learned was not true. If you break down anyone co-commercial or anyone Apple or anyone ad they obviously want to sell to everybody but who are they targeting in that message. Are they targeting the people who see themselves as creative. Are they targeting the people who see themselves as active. The same thing has to be true in your advertising. The message has to be targeting someone which then leads right into I think the next most common question we get is but you know but if we come up if I write this avatar John I'm excluding everybody else but that one only that one fat middle aged golfer with the bad back. It sure could help him but could help other people. So we tell you like how just by targeting the one person does not mean you're wiping out everybody else. Exactly what what you're after is the sweet spot. I know that's if you're going after males for example in your market there are females too. There are people who are richer they're poorer they're taller they're shorter they're married they're not married all these other things come into come into play but the thing is you want to try to reach that sweet spot. The average avatar the average person the average age the average gender the average demographic situation where they live what their economic social economic status is all of that stuff. If you find the sweet spot then your message can radiate out from there just because you're reaching somebody who is in a certain situation who represents most of who you're going after people who are more of that situation or less than that situation are still going to get the message. People who are maybe you know not as motivated by money are still going to be interested in getting the value the better deal all of those things that that that come into play. So find the sweet spot everything else kind of radiates out from that does that make sense. Yeah I think so and I to refer back to the example that you from one of the ads that you wrote that again had a great long run by writing to the person with the worst possible thing on two wheels. Yes that that message would still appeal to people who just in any way saw themselves as not keeping up with the the people at the top who were throwing seemingly endless money and resources at this. Actually what that got after was the sense that even if you could afford better equipment even if you had vast resources there's a sense in that market that you're being taken advantage of by the the people out there who were selling you the latest bike the latest helmet the latest safety gear just because that was their business to sell you more stuff. So it was refreshing to hear somebody say no you really don't need all of that backup go to basics and that message reverberated with people who actually weren't in that specific situation where they needed to go cheaper. They just thought you know what I even if I don't go cheaper I it's nice knowing that's there because this was an information product about getting faster time. So okay great okay another common question bit of feedback we get from students who have been to the program is I don't know like I either I have existing clients but I never surveyed them I don't know their age or this is a new product I have no idea. Accurate but yeah like how do they get themselves on the road to start figuring this out. What should they do if they're in that situation where their honest answer is I don't know if they're male or female or young or old. Well first find out you know that's that's the easy thing is just start asking around. The other thing is that get the product into people's hands. I mean you there is a logical way that you can figure out who the audience is in a general way. If you're selling weight loss information you're not going to go after skinny people for example. I mean that's that's an obvious one. If you're going after dog owners you're not going to go after after cat owners for example. So so just by you if you have to get the product into the hands of people you know your family find out what they say start to start to get an idea. It really is hard to believe that somebody could have no idea who is going to be the target audience. Although I've encountered clients where they had no idea mostly what they had was a misconception of who was going to buy the product. They they were misinformed about what the average prospect was in that particular market. They were misinformed about the socioeconomic status or the amount of money they had available or how much they were willing or not willing to spend on a particular product or service. So getting more hip to this stuff is essential to be able to write a better sales message targeted to the person you're after. So basically it's like find out. Yeah there you go. That message is going to come up a few other times. Yes. The rest of the program is like if you don't know stop like that's a bad sign. That's a sign of a you haven't thought this all the way through yet. You need to find out. You need to stop whatever else you think you should be doing next. And there's always a lot to do when you're a small business owner or you're a copyright or juggling client. If you don't know the answer to not just this is the first one of many things that to come put the brakes on and you need to get the answer to this. Yes. OK. Another question that's the last question we'll do this round that's common but doesn't apply to everybody but the people it applies to it's extremely important to is what if I don't think I'm selling to a person. I think I'm selling to a business. How do I then how do I not just my avatar and this is going to flow through the rest of the course. But what about that. How is that credibly or isn't that just so much different. None of this applies. So what you're talking about is what we call B2B or business to business marketing as opposed to B2C which is business to consumer marketing. So in B2B the common mistake that people make when they're creating a message to go out to their audience is they think I'm writing to IBM. I'm writing to you know a corporate entity and corporations are I'm sorry to say they're not actual people. People run these things. Who you're who you're trying to reach is an actual person who can make a decision to buy or not. Who can write the check to be able to purchase what you have or not. Who can facilitate whatever it is you want to initiate whether it's services or buying products or whatever. So when you're going to a business the idea is not to approach it as some faceless monolith robot structure but realize who it is that's actually going to be making decisions. These are people who are just like the avatars of B2C products or services which which mean that they have needs. They have fears. They have places that hurt. They may be broke. They may be looking for investors. They may be looking for more customers. There's a lot of things going on. Their job is at stake. There's all kinds of things. So you get into the head of the person you're going to be reaching. If it's in a particular kind of business you may be reaching the office managers for a wide swath of business. You may be reaching the VP of marketing. You may be having to reach the CEO or the guy who actually owns the company. They have fears and there are things keeping them up late at night and there are conversations constantly rotating in their head about what they need, what's going wrong, what's going right and how you can join that conversation. So you're always dealing with the human being. The human being is going to make the final decisions, the interim decisions and the initial decisions that even start to pay attention to what it is you're offering. Yeah, I think those are all excellent points. Part of my background years ago was I did sell to large companies like ARCO and Starbucks and American Airlines. What I can share out of that experience is everything John said is true. Remember there's an individual, there are people. However, this process, the sales process and most of what the simple writing system is going to focus on is selling to a person. You're reaching the person who can make the decision, take out the credit card or write the check. If your sales process is more complicated than that, you need to break it down. There are still a series of people who you want to take a specific response and maybe you need more than one response. You may need to first get through the office manager. So thus you need an ad that is written to that avatar because perhaps the real world of your process is you have to sell that person on an action which could be to bring your concept, your product, your service to a decision maker. At which point you may want to then have either you're telling the office manager or the decision maker, there's another action which is then you want to put the information that's appropriate to the decision maker. So if you're going to a large business where you do have committees and things like that involved and you're selling huge ticket items, break it down and then each one of those steps apply the simple writing system to. Yes. That seem fair? Yes. Now this is the time to dive into your exercise. Again, every section you'll have a chance to apply what you're learning to start building your ad. This is the time I want to tell you something that we run into with not most students but enough. I want you to remember the role of your coach. If you're in the coaching program and if you're not, you might want to be because if you're not in the coaching program as I said before, do the exercise anyway. They're critical. They're going to help you cement each thing and you're going to see, you're going to build as you move along. If you're in the coaching program, this is the time where you may not get the answer you were hoping from your coach. We don't hire the coaches with the job instruction of make you feel good about yourself and just reinforce your brilliance on the topic. We hire them to give you honest feedback. So that means sometime in this process, almost certainly, you're going to post something that you're pretty proud of and you're pretty sure nailed it and your coach is going to give you a straight out, no you missed or not so good. You really need to hit this hard. You need to be more specific. Please take that as constructive feedback and remember that's what you're paying for. It's not a sign of failure or that you're not doing well or you're not getting it. If you have to do an exercise a couple times before your coach says, yes, that's good, move on. So just keep that in mind. Do not become discouraged. The best teacher you ever had angered you at least one day in your life. Hopefully this will be another one of those experiences. Agreed? Yes, agreed. Okay, let's go to the exercise. What I want you to do is think about the person you're writing to. Who are you selling to? Where does he or she live? What are their average demographic ranges? As best as you can tell. And what are their needs? Where does it hurt? And what does she or he need to hear from you to feel good about buying? In other words, what are the problems that they have that you either through your service or your product are going to solve? This is very much like the bar room conversation that you may have encountered if you took the express course to get into this. If not, if you didn't go through that, let me explain it very quickly. The situation that I describe is let's say you're in a bar or a coffee shop and you're sitting there minding your own business and somebody comes up and sits down next to you and says to the bartender or the person handling the counter, not to you. They say, wow, pour me a double or I've had a horrible day and I just really want to have a drink and get out of here. And the bartender says to them, not to you, says, wow, Joe, you look kind of terrible. What's going on? And Joe says exactly what the situation is that your product or service fixes. So think about this situation because this is preparatory of you actually turning them and saying, wow, I hate to interrupt, but what I have actually addresses all of the situation you just described. The person may say, I need money for my son's college education. I don't know where I'm going to get it or the boss is going to fire me and I'm not sure what I'm going to do if I lose my job or my car is old and in the shop again, I need to get a new car or I have a dog that won't behave and I need to learn how to fix his misbehavior or I would love to be able to golf better. Anything, whatever they say, whatever has driven them to the point where they're actually describing their situation, think about that. Think about how what you have fits into there. They're going to describe their needs. They're going to describe where it hurts and they're going to help you understand what it is they need to hear from you to feel good about taking the next step, which is joining your program or buying from you or leaving a name so they get more information. Whatever the next step is, think about that one-to-one human conversation that's going on where they're actually describing that. What that situation does is help you with empathy. It helps you understand from their point of view, not from your point of view, not you saying, hey, if you have this problem, I can help you, but rather listening to them talk and how are they describing what's going on? How are they verbalizing what's wrong with them? So think about what it is that you offer and what the perfect prospect is and what is the situation that makes them a perfect prospect? What hurts? What's wrong? What is it that is going on in their lives that your product solves, addresses, or helps?
